sections.title: Integration with other family routines
sections.markdown: We pair movement with contextual anchors: after dinner, before screen time; after soccer practice; before bed for a calming walk. Anchors reduce decision fatigue. If our family's evening routine includes a 7:30 p.m. snack, we shift the walk to 8:00 p.m. so hunger doesn't derail the plan.

sections.title: Budgeting time and calories
sections.markdown: For adults, 15 minutes brisk walking ≈ 100–150 kcal burned (depending on weight and pace). For a household deciding whether to trade 15 minutes of TV for a walk, consider that over a week, replacing three 15‑minute TV sessions with walks adds ~450–900 kcal expenditure. Over a month, small but meaningful.

sections.title: Children's development benefits
sections.markdown: For children, daily movement supports motor development, sleep, and attention. The WHO recommends 60 minutes of moderate‑to‑vigorous activity per day for school‑age children, but practical increments matter: a 10–15 minute walk combined with play pushes toward that total.

sections.title: Building habit cues
sections.markdown:
Habits form around cues. We select stable cues: the end of dinner, the sound of the 8:15 p.m. reminder, the act of putting on shoes. We strengthen cues by pairing them with rewards: short talk time, a story, or a sticker. We keep rewards immediate; delayed rewards reduce learning speed.

We assumed reminders alone would be sufficient → observed that reminders plus a social conversation were more impactful → changed to combining a Brali reminder with a short evening "what went well" conversation.

sections.title: When to seek help
sections.markdown:
If adherence drops dramatically, and you want to maintain the goal, consider these steps:
- Reduce target by 20% for a week and re‑engage.
- Ask a neutral third party (friend or grandparent) to prompt one activity.
- If health issues are involved, check with a clinician on how to adjust intensity/time.

Check‑in Block
Use this block in Brali LifeOS or copy to paper. Keep answers short; fill them nightly for 14 days.

Daily (3 Qs):
- How many steps (or minutes) did you log today? (number)
- What was the small highlight? (1 sentence)
- Did anything block you from moving? (one reason)

Weekly (3 Qs):
- How many of the 7 days met the target? (count)
- What logistic change helped most? (1 sentence)
- What one change should we make for next week? (1 sentence)

Metrics:
- Primary: Family total steps per day (count)
- Secondary (optional): Minutes of purposeful activity per person (minutes)

We encourage logging both metrics when possible. Family total is easy to compare; minutes per person gives a finer view.

One‑page 5‑minute audit for Day 7
If you want a quick mid‑trial audit, do this in five minutes:
- Count how many green dots you had (0–7).
- Note one logistic friction that recurred.
- Decide one change for next week (target ±10%, change role, or swap a day).

Alternative path for busy days (≤5 minutes)
On busy days use the "micro‑cluster." Everyone spends five minutes doing step‑in‑place, marches, or quick stair climbs. Log as +500 adult steps and +400 child steps. Repeat this cluster up to three times a day if needed.

That pivot reduces friction and makes tonight's micro‑task the actual beginning.

sections.title: How to finish Day 14
sections.markdown: On Day 14, gather for 10 minutes. Answer the weekly questions and decide whether to continue, adjust, or pivot to a new modality. Record the final numbers in Brali and celebrate with a small ritual (a family photo, a special snack, or choosing next month's family outing).

sections.title: Common measurement mistakes and fixes
sections.markdown:
- Mistake: forgetting to log. Fix: set a phone-based reminder and make the data lead responsible.
- Mistake: device error. Fix: accept minutes and convert at 10 minutes = 1,000 adult steps.
- Mistake: comparing different ages directly. Fix: use per person percentages (e.g., +30% above baseline) instead of raw steps.

- A small jar of theme slips for variation.

sections.title: Permissions and social dynamics
sections.markdown: Families must grant permission to try and fail. We encourage a non‑judgmental approach: call failures data, not moral failing. When someone misses a day, ask a curiosity question: "What would help you do it tomorrow?" That phrasing invites solutions.

sections.title: Scaling beyond the family
sections.markdown: If the family goal works, consider inviting another family for one walk per week. Social scaling creates more accountability and increases novelty.
sections.html: If the family goal works, consider inviting another family for one walk per week. Social scaling creates more accountability and increases novelty.

sections.title: Evidence note (short)
sections.markdown:
Regular moderate activity improves mood and sleep within days and reduces risk factors for chronic disease over months to years. In behavior trials, shared social goals increase adherence by 20–40% compared to solo plans.

Mini‑FAQ
Q: Do we need fancy trackers? A: No. Phones suffice; otherwise use minute estimates. Q: What if a child refuses? A: Offer choice and rotation of roles. Q: What if we have uneven desire? A: Track both per person and family total; allow opt‑outs.

sections.title: Closing lived scene
sections.markdown:
On Day 3 at dusk, we recall another small scene: the evening walk turned into a 10‑minute detour to an ice cream shop because someone had an unexpected reward. We debated whether that broke the plan, then noticed that the conversation on the walk had been unusually deep — the kind that led to mentioning a school worry out loud. The reward and the physical activity both mattered. That balance — movement plus relationship time — is the hidden value of family fitness goals.

We end where we began: making a small decision tonight changes tomorrow's shape. Set the 14‑day microtrial, pick a target, start with one micro‑task, and make the check‑in the ritual that keeps you honest. The goal is not perfection; it is a sequence of small, visible, shared choices.

howTo.title: Define the metric baseline
howTo.summary: Each family member measures their typical daily steps using a device or estimation. Record these numbers to establish a baseline for setting achievable fitness goals.

howTo.title: Set a family fitness goal
howTo.summary: Gather as a family to choose a specific, measurable fitness target, such as daily steps or a weekly activity. Ensure everyone agrees and understands their roles.

howTo.title: Implement the 14-day microtrial
howTo.summary: Start the fitness challenge by engaging in daily activities that align with your chosen goal. Keep it simple and fun to encourage participation from all family members.

howTo.title: Reflect and adjust
howTo.summary: After a week, evaluate the progress as a family. Discuss what worked, what didn’t, and make necessary adjustments to the goals or activities to enhance engagement.

howTo.title: Check in with LifeOS Brali app
howTo.summary: Log your daily results and reflections in the Brali LifeOS app to track progress and maintain accountability. Access it at https://metalhatscats.com/life-os/family-fitness-challenge-tracker/.
